main_paper.raw: {"methods":["ProtoLLM: an example-free framework for tabular data classification using LLMs as prototype builders","Feature-level value generation by LLMs based on task and feature description, not examples","Prototype construction by combining LLM-generated feature values and few-shot examples via a fusion strategy","Classification by comparing test sample's Euclidean distance to class prototypes (training-free)"],"problems":["Overfitting and knowledge disruption in LLMs when using few-shot examples for tabular data modeling","Degradation of LLM reasoning ability due to noisy or poor-quality examples in few-shot tabular learning","Effective utilization of LLMs for zero-shot and few-shot tabular data classification without reliance on examples"],"datasets":["Multiple tabular datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy (implied by context)"],"results":[{"metric":"Performance compared to FeatLLM","value":"ProtoLLM outperforms FeatLLM by a clear margin (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes an example-free framework (ProtoLLM) for tabular data classification using LLMs, avoiding the use of few-shot examples in prompts","Introduces feature-level value generation by LLMs based solely on task and feature description, not on examples","Presents a training-free pipeline with no learnable variables required","Designs a fusion strategy to combine LLM-generated prototypes with few-shot examples for improved generalizability","Demonstrates that disentangling prior knowledge from target examples mitigates misalignment and improves few-shot learning"]}

methods: ProtoLLM: an example-free framework for tabular data classification using LLMs as prototype builders
Feature-level value generation by LLMs based on task and feature description, not examples
Prototype construction by combining LLM-generated feature values and few-shot examples via a fusion strategy
Classification by comparing test sample's Euclidean distance to class prototypes (training-free)
problems: Overfitting and knowledge disruption in LLMs when using few-shot examples for tabular data modeling
Degradation of LLM reasoning ability due to noisy or poor-quality examples in few-shot tabular learning
Effective utilization of LLMs for zero-shot and few-shot tabular data classification without reliance on examples
datasets: Multiple tabular datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)

results.value: ProtoLLM outperforms FeatLLM by a clear margin (exact numbers not provided in the excerpt)

novelty_claims: Proposes an example-free framework (ProtoLLM) for tabular data classification using LLMs, avoiding the use of few-shot examples in prompts
Introduces feature-level value generation by LLMs based solely on task and feature description, not on examples
Presents a training-free pipeline with no learnable variables required
Designs a fusion strategy to combine LLM-generated prototypes with few-shot examples for improved generalizability
Demonstrates that disentangling prior knowledge from target examples mitigates misalignment and improves few-shot learning

methods: Generative Tabular Learning (GTL): continued pre-training of large language models (LLMs) on diverse tabular data formatted in an instruction-oriented language
Instruction-oriented data templates for converting tabular data into text for LLM consumption
In-context learning and prompt-based zero-shot generalization for tabular tasks using LLMs (specifically LLaMA-2)
problems: Lack of universal tabular models that can generalize, transfer knowledge, and follow human instructions across domains
Current tabular deep learning models' limited zero-shot and in-context learning capabilities
Difficulty for LLMs to understand domain-specific tabular data, especially numerical features and statistical dependencies
Existing methods' reliance on supervised pre-training and fine-tuning, limiting adaptability and generalization
datasets: 384 public tabular datasets (176 classification, 208 regression) from various industrial domains
Holdout evaluation on 20 classification and 24 regression tasks
metrics: Regression metrics (not specified, but likely include RMSE, MAE, or R^2)

results.metric: Zero-shot and in-context learning performance (classification, ≤32 samples)
results.value: LLaMA-GTL achieves state-of-the-art performance, outperforming traditional models and rivaling GPT-4 in many tasks
LLaMA-GTL achieves greater improvements over existing fine-tuning baselines and narrows the gap with GPT-4
Demonstrated effective knowledge transfer and robust performance across domains

novelty_claims: Introduction of Generative Tabular Learning (GTL), a new paradigm that brings prompt-based zero-shot and in-context learning to tabular deep learning using LLMs
Continued pre-training of LLMs on instruction-oriented tabular data to enhance understanding of tabular features, numerical sequences, and statistical dependencies
Development of a data construction pipeline to convert tabular data into instruction-oriented language format for LLMs

Demonstration that LLaMA-GTL can achieve state-of-the-art zero-shot and in-context performance on both classification and regression tasks without fine-tuning, surpassing traditional models and even GPT-4 in some cases

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Prompt to Transfer (P2T): a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to extract transferable knowledge from source tabular datasets by identifying important column features and creating pseudo-demonstrations for in-context learning (ICL) prompts.","Prompting LLMs with pseudo-demonstrations generated from unlabeled or heterogeneous source tabular data."],"problems":["Transfer learning in tabular data, especially when source and target datasets are heterogeneous (different columns/features).","Learning with limited labeled data in tabular domains (few-shot and zero-shot scenarios).","Mitigating label scarcity in tabular machine learning tasks."],"datasets":["Various tabular learning benchmarks (specific datasets not named in the provided content, but examples include credit risk assessment and medical datasets such as diabetes and COVID-19 prediction)."],"metrics":["Performance improvement over baselines (e.g., accuracy, but specific metrics not named in the provided content)."],"results":[{"metric":"Performance improvement over previous methods","value":"P2T significantly and consistently outperforms existing methods, including self-supervised and unsupervised meta-learning methods, on various tabular learning benchmarks."}],"novelty_claims":["First to propose a tabular transfer learning framework (P2T) that leverages LLMs' in-context learning capabilities for tabular data.","P2T can utilize unlabeled or heterogeneous source tabular data by generating pseudo-demonstrations for LLM prompts.","Demonstrates that LLMs can be effective for tabular transfer learning, especially when source and target datasets have different formats.","Shows that converting tabular data into text and using LLMs can address the limited labeled data problem in tabular domains."]}

methods: Prompt to Transfer (P2T): a framework that uses large language models (LLMs) to extract transferable knowledge from source tabular datasets by identifying important column features and creating pseudo-demonstrations for in-context learning (ICL) prompts.
Prompting LLMs with pseudo-demonstrations generated from unlabeled or heterogeneous source tabular data.
problems: Transfer learning in tabular data, especially when source and target datasets are heterogeneous (different columns/features).
Learning with limited labeled data in tabular domains (few-shot and zero-shot scenarios).
Mitigating label scarcity in tabular machine learning tasks.
datasets: Various tabular learning benchmarks (specific datasets not named in the provided content, but examples include credit risk assessment and medical datasets such as diabetes and COVID-19 prediction).
metrics: Performance improvement over baselines (e.g., accuracy, but specific metrics not named in the provided content).

results.value: P2T significantly and consistently outperforms existing methods, including self-supervised and unsupervised meta-learning methods, on various tabular learning benchmarks.

novelty_claims: First to propose a tabular transfer learning framework (P2T) that leverages LLMs' in-context learning capabilities for tabular data.
P2T can utilize unlabeled or heterogeneous source tabular data by generating pseudo-demonstrations for LLM prompts.
Demonstrates that LLMs can be effective for tabular transfer learning, especially when source and target datasets have different formats.
Shows that converting tabular data into text and using LLMs can address the limited labeled data problem in tabular domains.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["FeatLLM: a novel in-context learning framework that uses LLMs as feature engineers for tabular data","Structured prompt design for LLMs to infer feature-target relationships and generate rule-based features","Binary feature creation from LLM-generated rules","Use of simple downstream models (e.g., linear regression) on engineered features","Ensemble approach with feature bagging to improve robustness and mitigate prompt size limitations"],"problems":["Few-shot tabular learning with limited labeled data","High inference cost and computational inefficiency of LLM-based tabular prediction methods","Dependence on LLM fine-tuning and training codebase access for tabular learning","Prompt size limitations in LLM-based tabular learning when handling many features"],"datasets":["13 different tabular datasets from a wide range of domains (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Classification accuracy (implied by 'outperforming' and '10% on average' improvement)"],"results":[{"metric":"Average performance improvement over TabLLM and STUNT","value":"10%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes FeatLLM, a framework that uses LLMs for feature engineering rather than end-to-end prediction in tabular learning","Eliminates the need for LLM inference at test time for each sample, reducing inference cost","Requires only API-level access to LLMs, not fine-tuning or training codebase access","Mitigates prompt size limitations via ensemble feature bagging","Demonstrates significant performance improvements (10% on average) over existing LLM-based tabular learning methods"]}

methods: FeatLLM: a novel in-context learning framework that uses LLMs as feature engineers for tabular data
Structured prompt design for LLMs to infer feature-target relationships and generate rule-based features
Binary feature creation from LLM-generated rules
Use of simple downstream models (e.g., linear regression) on engineered features
Ensemble approach with feature bagging to improve robustness and mitigate prompt size limitations
problems: Few-shot tabular learning with limited labeled data
High inference cost and computational inefficiency of LLM-based tabular prediction methods
Dependence on LLM fine-tuning and training codebase access for tabular learning
Prompt size limitations in LLM-based tabular learning when handling many features
datasets: 13 different tabular datasets from a wide range of domains (specific names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Classification accuracy (implied by 'outperforming' and '10% on average' improvement)

results.metric: Average performance improvement over TabLLM and STUNT

novelty_claims: Proposes FeatLLM, a framework that uses LLMs for feature engineering rather than end-to-end prediction in tabular learning
Eliminates the need for LLM inference at test time for each sample, reducing inference cost
Requires only API-level access to LLMs, not fine-tuning or training codebase access
Mitigates prompt size limitations via ensemble feature bagging
Demonstrates significant performance improvements (10% on average) over existing LLM-based tabular learning methods

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["STUNT (Self-generated Tasks from UNlabeled Tables): self-generating few-shot tasks by treating randomly chosen columns as target labels and using k-means clustering to generate pseudo-labels, with column perturbation to avoid trivial tasks","Meta-learning with Prototypical Networks on self-generated tasks","Unsupervised validation scheme using pseudo-validation sets generated from unlabeled data for hyperparameter search and early stopping"],"problems":["Few-shot learning on tabular data with limited labeled samples","Semi-supervised tabular learning with high annotation costs or difficulty in collecting new samples","Bridging the gap between self-supervised tasks and few-shot tasks in heterogeneous tabular data","Hyperparameter selection and early stopping without labeled validation sets in meta-learning for tabular data"],"datasets":["OpenML-CC18 benchmark"],"metrics":["Test accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Average test accuracy (1-shot)","value":"63.88% (STUNT) vs 59.89% (best baseline)"},{"metric":"Average test accuracy (5-shot)","value":"74.77% (STUNT) vs 72.19% (best baseline)"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes a new framework (STUNT) for few-shot semi-supervised tabular learning by self-generating tasks from unlabeled tables using column-based pseudo-labels and perturbations.","Introduces an unsupervised validation scheme for hyperparameter search and early stopping using pseudo-validation sets generated from unlabeled data.","Demonstrates that STUNT significantly outperforms prior unsupervised meta-learning, semi-supervised, and self-supervised baselines on few-shot tabular learning benchmarks.","Shows that STUNT is effective for multi-task learning scenarios, enabling adaptation to new tasks without retraining."]}

methods: STUNT (Self-generated Tasks from UNlabeled Tables): self-generating few-shot tasks by treating randomly chosen columns as target labels and using k-means clustering to generate pseudo-labels, with column perturbation to avoid trivial tasks
Meta-learning with Prototypical Networks on self-generated tasks
Unsupervised validation scheme using pseudo-validation sets generated from unlabeled data for hyperparameter search and early stopping
problems: Few-shot learning on tabular data with limited labeled samples
Semi-supervised tabular learning with high annotation costs or difficulty in collecting new samples
Bridging the gap between self-supervised tasks and few-shot tasks in heterogeneous tabular data
Hyperparameter selection and early stopping without labeled validation sets in meta-learning for tabular data
datasets: OpenML-CC18 benchmark

results.value: 63.88% (STUNT) vs 59.89% (best baseline)
74.77% (STUNT) vs 72.19% (best baseline)

novelty_claims: Proposes a new framework (STUNT) for few-shot semi-supervised tabular learning by self-generating tasks from unlabeled tables using column-based pseudo-labels and perturbations.
Introduces an unsupervised validation scheme for hyperparameter search and early stopping using pseudo-validation sets generated from unlabeled data.
Demonstrates that STUNT significantly outperforms prior unsupervised meta-learning, semi-supervised, and self-supervised baselines on few-shot tabular learning benchmarks.
Shows that STUNT is effective for multi-task learning scenarios, enabling adaptation to new tasks without retraining.

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Curated LLM (CLLM): Uses a frozen, black-box LLM to generate synthetic tabular data from a small real dataset, leveraging in-context learning rather than fine-tuning.","Post-generation data curation: Applies a principled curation mechanism based on learning dynamics, specifically using confidence and aleatoric uncertainty metrics, to select high-quality synthetic samples for downstream training."],"problems":["Data augmentation for tabular data in ultra low-data regimes (n < 100).","Limitations of traditional tabular data generators (e.g., GANs, VAEs, SMOTE) in low-data settings due to lack of diversity and accuracy in generated samples.","Computational and data inefficiency of fine-tuning LLMs for tabular data generation in low-data settings.","Need for systematic curation of synthetic data to improve downstream ML performance."],"datasets":["7 real-world tabular datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)"],"metrics":["Downstream model performance (e.g., accuracy, but specific metrics not detailed in the excerpt)","Confidence","Aleatoric uncertainty"],"results":[{"metric":"Downstream model performance","value":"CLLM outperforms 6 widely used tabular data generative models and data augmentation techniques on 7 real-world datasets in low-data regimes."},{"metric":"Effect of curation","value":"Curation mechanism improves downstream performance across generative models, with largest gains for underrepresented subgroups and in low-data settings."}],"novelty_claims":["First to combine LLM-based tabular data generation with a principled post-generation curation mechanism based on learning dynamics, confidence, and aleatoric uncertainty.","Demonstrates that curation of synthetic data is crucial and beneficial for downstream ML performance, especially in low-data regimes.","Shows that leveraging LLMs' prior knowledge via in-context learning (without fine-tuning) is effective for tabular data augmentation in ultra low-data settings.","Provides empirical evidence and insights into why the combination of LLM generation and curation is beneficial, especially for underrepresented subgroups."]}

methods: Curated LLM (CLLM): Uses a frozen, black-box LLM to generate synthetic tabular data from a small real dataset, leveraging in-context learning rather than fine-tuning.

Post-generation data curation: Applies a principled curation mechanism based on learning dynamics, specifically using confidence and aleatoric uncertainty metrics, to select high-quality synthetic samples for downstream training.
problems: Data augmentation for tabular data in ultra low-data regimes (n < 100).
Limitations of traditional tabular data generators (e.g., GANs, VAEs, SMOTE) in low-data settings due to lack of diversity and accuracy in generated samples.
Computational and data inefficiency of fine-tuning LLMs for tabular data generation in low-data settings.
Need for systematic curation of synthetic data to improve downstream ML performance.
datasets: 7 real-world tabular datasets (specific names not provided in the excerpt)
metrics: Downstream model performance (e.g., accuracy, but specific metrics not detailed in the excerpt)

results.value: CLLM outperforms 6 widely used tabular data generative models and data augmentation techniques on 7 real-world datasets in low-data regimes.
Curation mechanism improves downstream performance across generative models, with largest gains for underrepresented subgroups and in low-data settings.

novelty_claims: First to combine LLM-based tabular data generation with a principled post-generation curation mechanism based on learning dynamics, confidence, and aleatoric uncertainty.
Demonstrates that curation of synthetic data is crucial and beneficial for downstream ML performance, especially in low-data regimes.
Shows that leveraging LLMs' prior knowledge via in-context learning (without fine-tuning) is effective for tabular data augmentation in ultra low-data settings.
Provides empirical evidence and insights into why the combination of LLM generation and curation is beneficial, especially for underrepresented subgroups.

methods: Developed four different tests for memorization to detect if an LLM has seen a tabular dataset during training
Compared few-shot learning performance of LLMs on datasets seen during training versus datasets released after training

problems: Data contamination and memorization in LLMs when applied to tabular data

results.value: 6 percentage points average drop

Robust mean relative error (MSCI World, 2022, GPT-4)
Performance on in-context statistical classification (novel datasets)
results.value: Better than random, but degrades as dimension increases

novelty_claims: First to systematically develop and apply multiple tests to detect memorization of tabular datasets in LLMs
Demonstrates that GPT-3.5 and GPT-4 have memorized many popular tabular datasets verbatim
Shows that memorization leads to overfitting and affects few-shot learning evaluations
Reveals that LLMs are robust to data transformations on novel datasets but not on memorized ones

novelty_claims: Proposes Example Gisting, a novel approach for training example encoders with an attention bottleneck for ICL example selection

selected_papers.raw: {"methods":["Masked Encoding for Tabular Data (MET): a reconstruction-based self-supervised learning method for tabular data using random masking and transformer encoders to learn positional embeddings for each coordinate, concatenation of all token embeddings for fine-tuning, and adversarial perturbations in the input space via gradient ascent on reconstruction loss"],"problems":["Learning effective self-supervised representations from tabular data (tabular-SSL)","Lack of generalizable augmentations for tabular data compared to other domains like images or text","Suboptimal performance of previous reconstruction-based SSL methods for tabular data"],"datasets":["Criteo","Permuted MNIST","Permuted FMNIST","Permuted CIFAR-10","Adult-Income","CovType"],"metrics":["Accuracy"],"results":[{"metric":"Accuracy improvement on Criteo over SOTA","value":"5%"},{"metric":"Average accuracy improvement over previous SOTA (across five datasets)","value":"3.2%"},{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over Gradient-boosted decision trees (on multiple benchmarks)","value":">20%"},{"metric":"Accuracy improvement over SOTA tabular-SSL methods (DACL, SubTab, VIME)","value":"up to 10%"}],"novelty_claims":["Proposes MET, a novel reconstruction-based SSL method for tabular data using random masking and transformer encoders to learn positional embeddings for each coordinate.","Introduces concatenation of all token embeddings (instead of average pooling or special output token) for fine-tuning, which improves generalization and accuracy on tabular data.","Incorporates adversarial perturbations in the input space via gradient ascent on reconstruction loss, combined with masked reconstruction loss, to improve representation quality.","Demonstrates that MET significantly outperforms previous SOTA methods for tabular-SSL, including both contrastive and reconstruction-based approaches."]}

methods: Masked Encoding for Tabular Data (MET): a reconstruction-based self-supervised learning method for tabular data using random masking and transformer encoders to learn positional embeddings for each coordinate, concatenation of all token embeddings for fine-tuning, and adversarial perturbations in the input space via gradient ascent on reconstruction loss
problems: Learning effective self-supervised representations from tabular data (tabular-SSL)

results.value: up to 10%

novelty_claims: Proposes MET, a novel reconstruction-based SSL method for tabular data using random masking and transformer encoders to learn positional embeddings for each coordinate.
Introduces concatenation of all token embeddings (instead of average pooling or special output token) for fine-tuning, which improves generalization and accuracy on tabular data.
Incorporates adversarial perturbations in the input space via gradient ascent on reconstruction loss, combined with masked reconstruction loss, to improve representation quality.
Demonstrates that MET significantly outperforms previous SOTA methods for tabular-SSL, including both contrastive and reconstruction-based approaches.

methods: Few-Shot Transformer based Enrichment (FeSTE) framework
Adaptation of BERT for tabular data enrichment
Novel fine-tuning process for pre-trained language models (few-shot learning)
Reformulation of tabular tuples as sentences for transformer input
problems: Enrichment of tabular datasets using unstructured external data
Lack of generic, automated solutions for external enrichment of datasets (EED)
Difficulty of feature engineering with limited data (few-shot scenarios)
Inefficiency and dataset-specificity of existing enrichment methods
datasets: 17 diverse tabular datasets (not individually named in the provided content)

results.metric: Average improvement over baseline (with original features)
Average AUC (FeSTE alone, without original features)

novelty_claims: First generic and fully-automated approach for tabular data enrichment using unstructured external sources
Novel few-shot fine-tuning approach for transformer-based pre-trained language models, effective even with very small training sets
Ability to generate high-quality features from unstructured data and outperform existing BERT fine-tuning solutions
Reformulation of tabular data as sentences for transformer input
